topics.name: Political Theory and Concepts of Government
topics.description: JAMB UTME Section I: Definition of government, state, sovereignty, power, legitimacy, Rule of Law, and systems.

subtopics.name: Basic Political Concepts
objectives: Analyze government as an institution, process, and field of study
Distinguish state, nation, sovereignty, power, authority, legitimacy, political culture, and political socialization

subtopics.name: Systems of Government and Ideologies
objectives: Compare presidential, parliamentary, unitary, federal, confederal, monarchical, and republican systems
Distinguish capitalism, socialism, communism, fascism, and feudalism

topics.name: Organs of Government and Political Processes
topics.description: JAMB UTME Section II: Legislature, Executive, Judiciary, constitutions, political parties, and elections.

subtopics.name: Organs of Government and Rule of Law
objectives: Describe composition, functions, and relationships of Legislature, Executive, and Judiciary
Analyze Separation of Powers, Checks and Balances, Rule of Law, and Constitutionalism

subtopics.name: Elections, Political Parties, and Pressure Groups
objectives: Compare electoral systems: First-Past-The-Post, Proportional Representation, Absolute Majority
Describe political party systems, pressure groups, public opinion, and mass media roles in governance

topics.name: Constitutional History and Foreign Policy of Nigeria
topics.description: JAMB UTME Section III & IV: Pre-colonial systems, colonial administration, constitutions, federalism, and foreign policy.

subtopics.name: Pre-Colonial Systems and Constitutional History
objectives: Compare pre-colonial political systems in Hausa/Fulani, Yoruba, and Igbo societies
Analyze Clifford (1922), Richards (1946), Macpherson (1951), Lyttelton (1954), 1960, 1963, 1979, and 1999 Constitutions

subtopics.name: Nigerian Federalism and Foreign Policy
objectives: Analyze state creation, revenue allocation, local government reform, and civil service in Nigeria
Describe foreign policy principles, Afrocentricity, non-alignment, and membership in UN, AU, ECOWAS, Commonwealth
